definition: A set of properties of database transactions intended to guarantee data validity despite errors, power failures, and other mishaps.
example: Relational databases like PostgreSQL guarantee ACID transactions. If a transfer fails mid-way, the transaction is rolled back, preventing partial updates.

definition: A browser security mechanism that uses HTTP headers to tell browsers to give a web application running at one origin access to selected resources from a different origin.
example: If your frontend is on domain-a.com and calls domain-b.com/api, domain-b.com must return 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://domain-a.com' header.

definition: A workflow pipeline that automates the integration of code changes (testing, linting) and the automated deployment of verified changes to servers.
example: GitHub Actions or GitLab CI running unit tests on every pull request, then automatically releasing a new container image to production when merged.

definition: An attack that forces an authenticated user to execute unwanted actions on a web application in which they're currently authenticated.
example: An attacker emails a link with an image tag ''. If logged in, the browser makes the request.

definition: A geographically distributed group of servers that work together to provide fast delivery of Internet content like HTML, JS, CSS, and images.
example: Cloudflare or CloudFront caching static assets globally at edge nodes, reducing latency by delivering files from server close to the user's region.

definition: The phonebook of the Internet. It translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) to machine-readable IP addresses (like 142.250.190.46).
example: When navigating to opendevhub.com, your system queries a DNS resolver, which returns the server's IP address so your browser can connect.

definition: An open-source platform that enables developers to package, ship, and run applications in isolated environments called containers.
example: Writing a Dockerfile with instructions to copy your Node.js code, install node_modules, and run it on a specific Linux base image.

definition: A programming interface for web documents. It represents the page so that programs can change the document structure, style, and content.
example: Using JavaScript to interact with the DOM: 'document.getElementById("submit-btn").innerText = "Loading..."'.

definition: An architectural pattern in which changes to the application state are stored as a sequence of events, rather than just updating the current state in-place.
example: A banking ledger system. Instead of storing currentBalance = $1000, it stores: Deposit($500), Withdraw($200), Deposit($700). State is computed from the log.

definition: A query language for APIs and a runtime for fulfilling those queries with your existing data. It allows clients to request exactly what they need.
example: Client requests 'query { user(id: 1) { name email } }' and the server returns only the name and email, resolving over-fetching issues.

definition: A high-performance RPC framework developed by Google that uses HTTP/2 for transport and Protocol Buffers as the interface description language.
example: Microservices writing Protobuf files defining contracts. Service A calls Service B's functions directly over a fast, binary TCP connection.

definition: An open standard that defines a compact and self-contained way for securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object, signed cryptographically.
example: An Authorization header containing 'Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...'. The server decodes it using a secret key to verify user claims.

definition: An open-source container orchestration system for automating software deployment, scaling, and management.
example: Defining a Deployment manifest specifying 3 replicas of a Docker image, letting Kubernetes handle autoscaling, restarts, and rollouts.

definition: An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of small, autonomous, loosely coupled services that communicate over APIs.
example: An e-commerce platform splitting its codebase into Auth Service, Inventory Service, Payments Service, and Delivery Service.

definition: An industry-standard protocol for authorization that enables applications to obtain limited access to user accounts on an HTTP service, like Google or GitHub.
example: A website showing a 'Log in with GitHub' button. It redirects to GitHub, asks user for permission, and returns an access token to the site.

definition: An architectural style for designing networked applications. It relies on a stateless, client-server communication protocol, almost always HTTP.
example: An API exposing endpoints like 'GET /users/123' to fetch user data, and 'POST /users' to create a new user, utilizing HTTP verbs.

fullForm: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
definition: A mnemonic acronym for five design principles intended to make software designs more understandable, flexible, and maintainable.
example: Single Responsibility: A class should have only one reason to change (e.g. an InvoicePrinter class separate from Invoice calculation).

definition: SQL databases are relational, table-based, schema-driven, and scale vertically. NoSQL databases are non-relational, document/key-value based, dynamic-schema, and scale horizontally.
example: PostgreSQL (SQL) storing users in tables with strict foreign keys vs. MongoDB (NoSQL) storing user profiles as flexible JSON-like documents.

definition: The ability of a web application to render the HTML markup on the server instead of rendering it in the browser's client-side runtime.
example: Next.js rendering a page with dynamic DB data on the server, responding with pre-built HTML, which improves SEO and initial page load speed.

definition: A web application rendering pages directly in the browser's DOM using client-side JavaScript. The server sends a bare-bones HTML page with JS bundles.
example:
A standard Create React App loading index.html containing '
' and rendering the entire page after loading a bundle.js file.

definition: A communications protocol providing full-duplex communication channels over a single TCP connection, allowing real-time client-server communication.
example: A chat app client opening a connection: 'const socket = new WebSocket("wss://...")' and receiving instant messages from server without polling.

definition: A client-side process where JavaScript loads in the browser, attaches event listeners to pre-rendered HTML supplied by the server, making it interactive.
example: Next.js page loads HTML with a static '<button>Click</button>'. Hydration attaches a 'onClick' listener to that button in the DOM.

definition: An optimization technique that limits the rate at which a function gets triggered by delaying execution until a certain amount of idle time has elapsed.
example: A search input field calling an API. Debouncing ensures the API is called only after the user stops typing for 500ms.

definition: An optimization technique that limits the maximum number of times a function can be called over a given time interval.
example: Attaching a scroll event listener. Throttling ensures the listener runs at most once every 100ms, protecting scroll responsiveness.

definition: An optimization technique used primarily to speed up computer programs by storing the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result.
example: In React, using 'useMemo(() => computeExpensiveValue(a, b), [a, b])' to prevent recalculation on every state update unless dependency changes.

definition: The process of distributing network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server bears too much load, improving availability.
example: An NGINX server routing incoming traffic across 4 node app instances using a Round Robin algorithm, handling spikes without crash.

definition: The property of certain operations in mathematics and computer science whereby they can be applied multiple times without changing the result.
example: A POST request with an 'Idempotency-Key: 1234' header in Stripe. Even if called twice due to timeout, the customer is only charged once.

definition: A security vulnerability where an attacker manipulates SQL queries by injecting malicious SQL statements into entry fields.
example: Submitting 'admin' OR '1'='1' as a username to bypass password checks if queries are concatenated directly (without prepared statements).

definition: A security vulnerability where an attacker injects malicious client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users.
example: Saving a comment containing '<script>stealCookies(document.cookie)</script>' which runs in the browsers of all visitors reading that comment.

definition: A network traffic management strategy that limits the number of requests a user or IP address can make to an API within a specific timeframe.
example: An API returning HTTP 429 Too Many Requests if a client sends more than 60 requests per minute to prevent brute-force attacks.

definition: A term commonly used in the JavaScript context for dead-code elimination. It relies on the static structure of ES2015 module syntax (import and export) to discard unused code during bundle compilation.
example: Using Webpack or Rollup: if you 'import { clone } from "lodash-es"', tree shaking ensures the bundle excludes all other unused Lodash functions, reducing payload sizes.

definition: A suite of different technologies allowing you to create reusable custom elements—with their functionality encapsulated away from the rest of your code—and utilize them in your web apps.
example: Creating a custom HTML tag '<custom-user-card>' using JavaScript CustomElementRegistry, templates, and the Shadow DOM.

definition: The mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking I/O operations, despite being single-threaded, by offloading operations to the system kernel whenever possible.
example: When `setTimeout` is called, it registers the callback in the Web APIs pool. Once finished, the callback is pushed to the Callback Queue and processed by the Event Loop once the execution stack is empty.

definition: Functions executed on the server side but triggered from the client-side component directly without declaring dedicated REST endpoints.
example: In Next.js: declaring 'use server' inside an action function, then calling it inside a form's `onSubmit` trigger to mutate DB values securely.

definition: A web standard that allows developers to attach a hidden, encapsulated DOM subtree to an element, keeping CSS styling and JavaScript isolated from the main document.
example: Browsers use Shadow DOM internally to render input controls like '<input type="range">'. Page-wide CSS styles won't bleed into or affect the slider controls.

definition: An initiative by Google to provide unified guidance for quality signals that are essential to delivering a great user experience on the web (LCP, FID, CLS).
example: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures loading performance: the time it takes for the page's main content to render (ideal is 2.5 seconds or less).

definition: The algorithm used by browsers to determine which CSS rule gets applied to an element when multiple rules conflict. It is calculated based on selector weight.
example: An inline style has higher specificity than an ID selector ('#id'). An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector ('.class').

definition: The sequence of steps the browser goes through to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into actual pixels on the screen.
example: Constructing DOM -> Constructing CSSOM -> Combining into Render Tree -> Layout calculations -> Painting pixels.

definition: A web application that interacts with the user by dynamically rewriting the current web page with new data from the web server, instead of loading entire new pages.
example: An application built in React or Vue where navigation happens instantly via client-side routing, loading only JSON payloads for data changes.

definition: A traditional web application structure where every navigational transition triggers the browser to request and download a new HTML page from the server.
example: A legacy WordPress or PHP site where clicking a menu link triggers a full browser reload and downloads a new index.php template.

definition: A theorem stating that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of three guarantees: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
example: In a network partition, a DB must choose either Consistency (returning errors or blocking to sync) or Availability (returning stale data from local replicas).

definition: A type of database partitioning that separates very large databases into smaller, faster, more easily managed parts, called data shards, spread across nodes.
example: Sharding a users table by country: storing European users on Database Server A and Asian users on Database Server B to optimize speed.

definition: A data structure technique (usually using B-Trees) that improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a database table at the cost of slower writes.
example: Adding an index on the email column: 'CREATE INDEX idx_user_email ON users(email);'. Select queries lookup records instantly rather than scanning entire table.

definition: A cache of database connections maintained so that connections can be reused when future requests to the database are required.
example: Instead of opening and closing a TCP socket on every single server request, the app borrows a pre-opened connection from a pool of 10 connections.

definition: A family of techniques for providing atomicity and durability in database systems. Changes are written to a secure log file before being applied to the DB pages.
example: In Postgres, transactions are appended to the WAL file first. If server power goes down, the database replays the log to recover pending transactions.

definition: A consistency model used in distributed computing where data updates eventually propagate to all nodes, guaranteeing they match given no new updates.
example: DNS updates. When modifying a domain's DNS record, the change does not sync everywhere immediately, but eventually updates across all servers.

definition: The frequent copying of data from a primary database on one server to replicas on other servers to create backups or balance read traffic.
example: A primary Postgres database handling all write operations, syncing data to 2 read replicas that serve GET requests, splitting query loads.

definition: An asynchronous service-to-service communication protocol used in serverless and microservices architectures to buffer and route message payloads.
example: Using RabbitMQ or Apache Kafka: a payment service publishes a 'payment_processed' event. The shipping service consumes it to pack items.

definition: A method of altering the behavior of a web page or application with custom callbacks. They are triggered by events and push payloads via HTTP.
example: Stripe sending a POST request to your `/api/stripe-webhook` endpoint to notify your server that a subscription has been successfully paid.

definition: A web development technique where the client requests information from the server and the server holds the request open until new data is available.
example: A simple chat app sending an AJAX request. The server waits for a message to arrive before replying, rather than returning empty JSON immediately.

definition: A server push technology enabling a client to receive automatic, real-time updates from a server over an HTTP connection, in one direction.
example: ChatGPT streaming the response text character-by-character into the browser using an EventSource connection rather than waiting for complete output.

definition: An API management tool that sits between a client and a collection of backend services, acting as a single entry point and reverse proxy.
example: Using Kong or AWS API Gateway to handle authentication, CORS pre-flights, rate limiting, and request routing before forwarding to microservices.

definition: A programming technique for converting data between incompatible type systems in databases and object-oriented programming languages.
example: Using Prisma: 'prisma.user.findMany({ where: { role: "ADMIN" } })' instead of writing raw SQL 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE role = 'ADMIN';'.

definition: A software design pattern commonly used for developing user interfaces that divides the program logic into three interconnected elements.
example: A Ruby on Rails app: Model manages database users, View renders HTML templates, Controller bridges requests between model query and view output.

definition: The process of managing and provisioning computer data centers through machine-readable definition files, rather than physical configuration.
example: Writing a Terraform or CloudFormation file specifying 2 virtual servers, a firewall, and an S3 bucket, creating the assets in AWS in one command.

definition: A workflow pattern that uses Git repositories as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application configurations, automating sync.
example: Updating a Kubernetes deployment file in Git. A tool like ArgoCD detects the commit and updates the live cluster automatically.

definition: A deployment strategy that reduces downtime and risk by running two identical production environments, only one of which is live at any time.
example: App version v1 runs on Environment Blue (live). Deploy v2 to Green (idle), test it, and toggle the Load Balancer to route users to Green.

definition: A deployment strategy where a new version of software is rolled out to a small subset of users before making it available to the entire userbase.
example: Routing 95% of traffic to the stable production app (v1) and 5% to the new release (v2) to test error logs and metrics on real users first.

definition: A cloud-computing execution model where the cloud provider acts as the server, dynamically managing the allocation and scaling of machine resources.
example: Using AWS Lambda or Vercel Functions. You upload a single JS function. The cloud runs it only when requested, billing you by execution milliseconds.

definition: A deployment pipeline setup where servers are updated to a new version without interrupting active client requests or closing service sockets.
example: Rolling updates in Kubernetes: spin up new pods of v2, wait for health check passes, and shut down v1 pods sequentially without request drops.

definition: A security practice for storing passwords. A salt (random string) is appended to the password, which is then run through a one-way hashing algorithm.
example: Password 'secret123' combined with random salt 'x82j' hashes to 'da83fb...' using bcrypt, preventing attackers from using precomputed rainbow tables.

fullForm: Secure Hypertext Transfer Protocol / Secure Sockets Layer
definition: An extension of HTTP that uses TLS (formerly SSL) to encrypt all communications between a client's browser and the web server, securing data in transit.
example: Navigating to a banking site. The browser shows a lock icon, encrypting password inputs so attackers on public Wi-Fi can't read payloads.

definition: An HTTP header that allows site operators to restrict the resources (such as JavaScript, CSS, Images) that the browser is allowed to load for a given page.
example: Setting a CSP header: 'default-src 'self' https://trusted.com'. The browser blocks scripts from untrusted external domains, mitigating XSS attacks.

definition: A malicious attempt to disrupt normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target with a flood of Internet traffic.
example: An attacker controlling 10,000 infected machines (botnet) to spam HTTP requests to a target API, exhausting its memory, causing it to crash.

definition: The practice of limiting access rights for users, applications, and system processes to only those permissions absolutely necessary to perform their work.
example: Creating an IAM database user that only has READ access to the database, rather than granting full admin permissions to the backend application.

definition: An identity verification layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol, allowing clients to verify the identity of the end-user using tokens.
example: Signing into a portal using Google. OIDC returns an ID Token (JWT) containing profile details like email and name alongside authorization access.

definition: A principle of software development aimed at reducing repetition of software patterns, replacing it with abstractions or data normalization.
example: Extracting duplicate database connection parameters scattered across 5 files into a single, shared database helper module.

definition: A principle of extreme programming that states a programmer should not add functionality until deemed absolutely necessary.
example: Refraining from building a complex, multi-currency wallet feature for an MVP site that only takes single dollar payments for now.

definition: A design principle stating that systems work best if they are kept simple rather than made complicated; unnecessary complexity should be avoided.
example: Using a standard relational table lookup rather than building a complex, distributed key-value caching system when database queries are already fast.

definition: A software architecture model where the user interface, backend application database accesses, and workers are packaged in a single, unified program.
example: A Ruby on Rails or Django project containing all tables, HTML rendering routes, and logic modules in one single code repository.

definition: A dedicated infrastructure layer built into an application to control service-to-service communications, managing security, routing, and logging.
example: Using Istio or Linkerd to handle automatic TLS encryptions, retries, and network telemetry logs between microservices in a cluster.

definition: A system design quality that ensures a pre-agreed level of operational performance (usually uptime) is maintained during a given period.
example: A multi-region setup with automatic failover. If the AWS US-East region fails, DNS switches traffic to US-West, keeping the site online.

definition: The property that enables a system to continue operating properly in the event of the failure of one or more of its internal components.
example: An application cluster running 5 node replicas. If 2 servers crash due to hardware issues, the other 3 continue running the application without interruption.

definition: A widely adopted versioning system for software releases defined as: MAJOR version for breaking API changes, MINOR version for backwards-compatible features, and PATCH for backwards-compatible bug fixes.
example: Upgrading a package from 2.4.1 to 2.5.0 adds new features without breaking existing code. Upgrading to 3.0.0 will require updating code for breaking changes.

definition: A flaw in an electronic or software system where the output is dependent on the sequence or timing of uncontrollable concurrent events.
example: Two threads checking userBalance ($100). Both withdraw $80 at the exact same millisecond. DB checks balance for both as $100, allowing a total withdrawal of $160.
